parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to bounce back from small mistakes without giving them a second thought, but lately I just sit with the heavy feeling until it settles in my chest. It has been dragging on for months now, and I still catch myself replaying every awkward conversation in my head. I know I am overthinking it, yet the embarrassment feels completely real and impossible to shake off. I just wish I could return to that lighter version of myself who never carried this much weight around.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a lot right now, and I want you to know that what you’re feeling is completely valid. That shift from brushing things off to feeling them so deeply can be disorienting and exhausting. It’s like your mind has started holding onto moments that used to just slip away, and that weight in your chest is real—it’s not just in your head.

It’s okay to miss that lighter version of yourself, and it’s also okay to acknowledge that you might be in a season where things feel heavier. Sometimes our minds start holding onto things more tightly when we’re stressed, tired, or going through changes—even if we can’t pinpoint exactly why.

If you’re open to a gentle suggestion: sometimes giving yourself permission to feel the embarrassment without judging it can help loosen its grip. You might try writing down those replaying conversations, not to analyze them, but just to let them exist outside of your head. Or, if it feels right, talking to someone you trust—or even a professional—can help lighten that load.

You’re not alone in this. Many people go through phases where they feel more sensitive to past moments, and it doesn’t mean you’ve lost that lighter part of yourself—it might just be resting for a while. Be kind to yourself in the meantime.

turns.user_message: I used to laugh off my own slip-ups, but now I spend hours dissecting every little thing I said wrong. This pattern has been sticking with me for so long that it feels like my default setting rather than a passing phase. I keep trying to convince myself that nobody else notices, but the quiet shame just keeps piling up inside me anyway. It is exhausting to constantly monitor my own behavior just to avoid feeling like a complete disappointment.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy emotional burden, and I want you to know that your feelings are completely valid. Many people experience this kind of self-scrutiny, but that doesn’t make it any less draining or painful for you.

What you’re describing—shifting from laughing things off to deeply analyzing every perceived mistake—can feel like a trap. It’s exhausting to be in a constant state of self-monitoring, especially when it’s fueled by a fear of disappointing others or yourself. That quiet shame you mentioned can feel isolating, but you’re not alone in feeling this way.

Sometimes, patterns like this develop as a way to protect ourselves—maybe from criticism, rejection, or past experiences where mistakes felt costly. But over time, that protective mechanism can start to feel more like a prison than a shield.

If you’re open to it, here are a few gentle questions or ideas that might help you explore this further:

- Have you noticed if there are specific situations or people that trigger this kind of self-criticism more intensely?
- What would it feel like to offer yourself the same kindness you might offer a friend who made a similar slip-up?
- Sometimes, writing down these thoughts can help—not to analyze them, but to release them from circling in your mind.

You don’t have to navigate this alone. If this pattern is impacting your well-being, speaking with a therapist or counselor could provide you with tools to shift away from this default setting toward something that feels more compassionate and sustainable.

You deserve peace with yourself, and it’s okay to seek support in finding it.

turns.user_message: I used to move through my days with a quiet confidence, but lately I feel like I am constantly apologizing for my own existence. This heavy sense of regret has been following me around for years, and it has completely warped how I see myself. Every single interaction leaves me convinced that I am fundamentally flawed and unworthy of basic respect. I hate that I let this lingering guilt dictate everything I do, because I know it is slowly eating away at who I actually am.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy emotional burden, and I want you to know that your feelings are valid. That sense of regret and self-doubt can be incredibly isolating, especially when it starts to shape how you move through the world.

You mentioned that this has been going on for years—have you ever had the chance to explore where this feeling might have started? Sometimes, understanding the root of these emotions can be a first step toward loosening their grip on you. It’s also important to remember that feeling flawed doesn’t mean you *are* flawed. Our minds can sometimes convince us of things that aren’t true, especially when we’ve been carrying guilt or shame for a long time.

You said you know this is eating away at who you actually are—that quiet confidence you used to have. That person is still there, even if it feels buried right now. It takes a lot of strength to recognize that something isn’t serving you anymore, and it’s okay to seek support, whether through talking with someone you trust or exploring ways to rebuild that sense of self-worth.

You deserve to move through your days without apologizing for your existence. You are worthy of respect, kindness, and compassion—especially from yourself.
